---
title: "Exercises: Eigenvalues and Diagonalization"
description: "Practice problems and worked solutions for eigenvalues, eigenvectors, diagonalization, and matrix powers."
page-type: exercise
status: published
update-status: current
level: foundation
topic: linear-algebra
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/eigenvalues-and-diagonalization.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Compute eigenpairs in simple examples."
  - "Check diagonalizability from available eigenvectors."
  - "Connect matrix powers to spectral modes."
applications:
  - dynamical-systems
  - graph-learning
keywords:
  - exercises
  - eigenvalues
  - eigenvectors
  - diagonalization
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-24
source-check-date: 2026-04-24
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/proofs/distinct-eigenvalues-give-independent-eigenvectors.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/labs/matrix-powers-and-spectral-modes.qmd
---

## Scope and Goals

This set trains four things:

- finding eigenvalues and eigenvectors in small examples
- checking whether diagonalization is available
- reading matrix powers through eigen-modes
- preparing for spectral applications and SVD

## Prerequisites

- matrix multiplication
- basis and independence
- diagonal matrices and powers

## Warm-Up Problems

1. Find the eigenvalues of

   $$
   \begin{bmatrix}
   2 & 0 \\
   0 & 3
   \end{bmatrix}.
   $$

2. Find one eigenvector of

   $$
   \begin{bmatrix}
   2 & 1 \\
   0 & 3
   \end{bmatrix}
   $$

   for each eigenvalue.

## Core Problems

1. Diagonalize

   $$
   A =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   4 & 0 \\
   0 & 1
   \end{bmatrix}
   $$

   and compute $A^5$.
2. Let

   $$
   P =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   0.8 & 0.2 \\
   0.2 & 0.8
   \end{bmatrix}.
   $$

   Find its eigenvalues and explain which mode decays.
3. Explain why a matrix with $n$ distinct eigenvalues is diagonalizable.

## Proof Problems

1. Prove that if $Av = \lambda v$ and $c \neq 0$, then $cv$ is also an eigenvector with eigenvalue $\lambda$.
2. Show that if $A = S \Lambda S^{-1}$, then $A^k = S \Lambda^k S^{-1}$ for every positive integer $k$.

## Computational or Applied Problems

1. Open [Computation Lab: Matrix Powers and Spectral Modes](../labs/matrix-powers-and-spectral-modes.qmd). Change the mixing parameter and describe how the second eigenvalue controls the rate of convergence.
2. In software, compute the eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix and compare them with the singular values of the same matrix when it is positive semidefinite.

## Hints

1. For diagonal matrices, the eigenvalues are already visible.
2. For matrix powers, diagonalize first and then power the diagonal entries.
3. Distinct eigenvalues give independent eigenvectors.

## Full Solutions

### Solution 1

The eigenvalues are $2$ and $3$.

### Solution 2

For $\lambda=2$, solve

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
v = 0,
$$

so one eigenvector is $(1,0)^\top$.

For $\lambda=3$, solve

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
-1 & 1 \\
0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
v = 0,
$$

so one eigenvector is $(1,1)^\top$.

### Solution 3

The matrix is already diagonal, so

$$
A = I
\begin{bmatrix}
4 & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
I^{-1}.
$$

Therefore

$$
A^5 =
\begin{bmatrix}
4^5 & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1024 & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

### Solution 4

The eigenvalues are $1$ and $0.6$.

The consensus mode $(1,1)^\top$ persists, while the disagreement mode $(1,-1)^\top$ decays because $0.6^k \to 0$.

### Solution 5

Distinct eigenvalues give linearly independent eigenvectors. So an $n \times n$ matrix with $n$ distinct eigenvalues has $n$ independent eigenvectors, which is exactly what diagonalization needs.

## Common Errors

- treating the zero vector as an eigenvector
- assuming repeated eigenvalues automatically prevent diagonalization
- forgetting that eigenvectors are determined only up to nonzero scaling

## What To Do Next

Open [SVD and Low-Rank Approximation](../concepts/svd-and-low-rank-approximation.qmd) next. That page brings spectral thinking back through the symmetric matrix $A^\top A$ and turns it into a stable approximation tool.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.06: Lecture 21, Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors](https://live.ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-06-linear-algebra-spring-2010/resources/lecture-21-eigenvalues-and-eigenvectors/) - `First pass` - official source for computational practice on eigenpairs. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [MIT 18.06SC: Lecture 22, Diagonalization and Powers of A](https://live.ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-06sc-linear-algebra-fall-2011/resources/lecture-22-diagonalization-and-powers-of-a-1/) - `Second pass` - official source for repeated powers. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [A Comprehensive Survey on Spectral Clustering with Graph Structure Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.13597) - `Paper bridge` - useful later for seeing eigen-modes inside graph methods. Checked `2026-04-24`.
